body: OpenAgents Desktop is a local-first workroom around your ordinary Codex session. It keeps the active conversation central while session navigation, tools, decisions, and repository review remain close and legible. The current macOS ARM64 build is a release candidate. A public download will follow release approval; this documentation does not turn candidate evidence into a release claim. What the workroom does Starts a new Codex conversation or resumes durable local history. Renders authored messages, plans, tools, decisions, changes, interruptions, and terminal outcomes in one causal timeline. Supports send, stop, steer, queue, questions, approvals, and plan review through typed commands. Shows bounded repository status and exact diffs without adding Git mutation controls. Restores interrupted work explicitly instead of silently rerunning it or substituting another session. What stays outside the MVP OpenAgents Desktop is not a full editor, interactive terminal, browser, payment product, or autonomous deployment system. ProductSpec, Fleet, and an OpenAgents account are not prerequisites for an ordinary local Codex conversation. Where to go next Run from source for the current developer path. Use the workroom for sessions, timeline, and composer behavior. Review changes for read-only repository review and recovery semantics. Understand the boundary for credential, renderer, and local-authority rules. Explore Future / Advanced for clearly labeled historical ideas and dormant design horizons.
description: A local-first workroom for durable, reviewable Codex work.
headings: What the workroom does What stays outside the MVP Where to go next
title: OpenAgents Desktop

body: Availability The accepted build is a signed and notarized macOS ARM64 release candidate. It is not a public release yet, so there is no supported public installer link in these docs. Run from source The repository pins Node 24.13.1 and pnpm 11.10.0 . From a clean clone: bash Copy pnpm install pnpm run dev:openagents-desktop Development uses the isolated OpenAgents Dev profile and can run beside an installed production application. Stop the development process with Control-C so both Electron and the local Vite server shut down. Verify the package The canonical package gate runs typechecking, tests, the production build, and Electron smoke coverage: bash Copy pnpm --dir apps/openagents-desktop run verify That gate uses privacy-safe fixtures rather than ambient Codex history. A successful test run is engineering evidence; it is not release publication. Start a conversation Open New session . Choose or confirm the repository context. Write the objective in the composer. Send the turn and follow the timeline. Resolve any question or approval in the focused decision surface. Open Review changes when the turn produces repository changes. OpenAgents uses the app-owned Codex runtime and ordinary logged-in Codex session. The initial local workflow does not require an OpenAgents account.
description: Run the current OpenAgents Desktop candidate from source.
headings: Availability Run from source Verify the package Start a conversation
title: Getting started

body: Sessions The session rail paints bounded metadata before a selected transcript finishes hydrating. Search, selection, archive, delete, and paging preserve stable identity and ordering. Switching away and back must return to the same session rather than creating an imitation of it. Timeline The center column is a causal timeline, not a loose activity feed. User and assistant messages are the primary transcript. Plans, reasoning summaries, tools, approvals, changes, and usage appear as compact work rows. Active streams update in place without moving a reader who has scrolled away from the live edge. Gaps, interruption, failure, and unavailable state remain visible and named. Finished adjacent work may fold into a bounded summary without hiding the authored conversation. Composer actions The composer exposes the command that is valid for the current turn: Send starts a turn while idle. Stop interrupts the active turn. Steer updates the active turn where the runtime supports it. Queue records the next instruction without pretending it changed the current turn. Keyboard and pointer entry points dispatch the same registered command. Optimistic UI never records a question, approval, or plan decision before the runtime accepts it. Decisions and blockers Questions, approvals, and plan reviews open as focused surfaces with complete choices and explicit pending, stale, failed, and resolved states. Escape and close behavior return focus predictably. A blocker stays a blocker until a real response resolves it.
description: Understand sessions, the causal timeline, and composer actions.
headings: Sessions Timeline Composer actions Decisions and blockers
title: Workroom

body: Repository review OpenAgents Desktop keeps repository context adjacent to the conversation. The review surface may show bounded status and an exact correlated diff for the active work context. The MVP review boundary is intentionally read-only. It does not expose discard, commit, branch, push, pull-request, merge, or arbitrary command execution controls. Review fails visibly when output is stale, revoked, secret-shaped, binary, oversized, or unavailable. Absolute filesystem paths and host credentials do not enter the renderer projection. Reload and restart Renderer reload and application restart reconcile against durable session and run identity. Confirmed work is restored from its authoritative record. An incomplete stream remains interrupted or pending reconciliation. A proven history gap triggers an authoritative refetch. No restart silently reruns provider work. No stale response may overwrite a newer selection or subscription generation. If the app cannot prove the current state, it should say so. Unknown pending reconciliation is safer and more truthful than optimistic completion. Recovery checklist Confirm the selected session and repository context. Read the latest interruption, gap, or connection row. Reopen the exact change review if repository state is still available. Resume only through the visible runtime action. Start a new session only when you intend to create new work identity.
description: Review exact changes and understand restart behavior.
headings: Repository review Reload and restart Recovery checklist
title: Review and recovery

body: Local-first operation The ordinary Desktop workroom remains useful without an OpenAgents account. Repository access and Codex execution are held by the desktop host, not by browser-style renderer code. Renderer boundary The renderer receives schema-checked projections and typed intent keys. It does not receive raw bearer tokens, provider credentials, Pylon control tokens, Node handles, process handles, arbitrary IPC channels, raw runtime events, or unrestricted filesystem access. Electron runs with context isolation, no renderer Node integration, sandboxing, deny-by-default permissions, restricted navigation, and a closed preload bridge. Codex custody The application uses its exact bundled Codex runtime rather than falling back to an arbitrary binary on PATH . Account details are bounded and obscured by default in settings. Sensitive values do not belong in logs, screenshots, public receipts, or docs. Repository data Repository status and diffs are scoped to the active work context. Secret-shaped, oversized, binary, stale, or revoked material fails closed. Review does not grant mutation authority. Account linking OpenAgents account linking is optional for the initial local workflow. When linked features are used, the host owns encrypted native-session custody. Credentials do not cross into the renderer or become part of the Codex transcript.
description: The local authority, credential, and renderer boundaries of OpenAgents Desktop.
headings: Local-first operation Renderer boundary Codex custody Repository data Account linking
title: Security and privacy

body: The development app does not open Confirm that the repository's pinned Node and pnpm versions are active, dependencies are installed, and port 5734 is free. Run the package verification gate before changing runtime policy: bash Copy pnpm --dir apps/openagents-desktop run verify Codex is unavailable or incompatible Open Settings and use the Codex-specific maintenance action. Desktop checks the app-owned bundled runtime; installing another global binary does not repair that authority. A failed update or re-probe remains failed until the exact bundled runtime passes. A turn appears interrupted Do not create a replacement session immediately. Read the interruption or connection row, confirm the selected session, and use the visible resume or retry action. Reloading the renderer must not cause duplicate execution. Review is unavailable The repository may have changed, the work context may be stale, or the requested output may have crossed a privacy or size bound. Refresh the active repository context and request a new bounded review rather than reaching around the host boundary. The sidebar or review drawer covers the conversation At narrow widths the session rail and review drawer intentionally become overlays. Close the overlay with its control or Escape. The conversation and composer should remain usable at the supported minimum window. Report a reproducible issue Open a GitHub issue with the application version, operating system, visible state, and reproduction steps. Remove credentials, absolute private paths, repository secrets, and private transcript content before attaching evidence.
description: Diagnose common development and workroom states without widening authority.
headings: The development app does not open Codex is unavailable or incompatible A turn appears interrupted Review is unavailable The sidebar or review drawer covers the conversation Report a reproducible issue
title: Troubleshooting

body: The docs build emits static agent-readable artifacts without running an AI endpoint or MCP server. Available artifacts llms.txt indexes the public documentation tree. llms-full.txt provides the complete public documentation corpus. agent-readability.json describes enabled static formats and content-use signals. Add .md to a documentation route to request its raw Markdown mirror. These artifacts contain only the curated public content under this docs package. They do not index the repository-wide docs/ directory, internal audits, runbooks, evidence, private traces, or operational topology. Authority boundary Instruction files and documentation help tools understand the system; they do not grant authentication, repository, deployment, payment, moderation, or operator authority. Server-side policy remains authoritative for every action. Ask AI and hosted MCP are disabled. Search is a local static Orama index built with the site. Product claim authority Product promises are machine-facing evidence, not human website navigation. Agents that need current claim state should read the structured projections directly: /api/public/product-promises is the current registry projection. /api/public/product-promises/transitions is the transition receipt stream. /api/public/product-promises/audit is the audit projection. Read the promise identifier, state, evidence, blockers, and caveat together. Documentation, a screenshot, a candidate receipt, or a ProductSpec does not independently make a capability publicly available. /docs/product-promises remains a compatibility redirect to this agent-facing boundary.
description: Static Markdown and discovery artifacts for tools and coding agents.
headings: Available artifacts Authority boundary Product claim authority
title: Agent-readable docs

body: Status: Historical context and dormant design horizon. This section is not part of the current MVP and is not a roadmap commitment. OpenAgents has explored a much larger system than the product shipping today. The current product is the local-first OpenAgents Desktop Codex workroom. The topics here explain earlier experiments and design ideas without presenting them as available features. How to read this section Historical means OpenAgents previously researched, demonstrated, or implemented some version of the idea. Removed means the accepted Desktop MVP deliberately excludes it and its executable product path was retired. Dormant design horizon means useful contracts or lessons remain, but no active product-expansion lane exists. A topic moves back into active development only through a new, bounded owner decision and issue. Old code, demos, transcripts, or tests do not create that authority. Topics Marketplaces covers agent, plugin, data, compute, and accepted-outcome markets. Nostr covers identity, registries, storage, and open job protocols. Bitcoin and Lightning covers L402, wallets, tipping, payouts, and settlement experiments. Remote workrooms covers Fleet, mobile supervision, managed targets, and portable sessions. Source boundary This section is a curated reading guide based on the canonical Sol roadmap and the public build-in-public transcript index. Transcripts record what was explored at the time; they do not prove present availability. The roadmap and current product evidence take precedence.
description: Historical OpenAgents ideas and dormant design horizons, separated from the current Desktop MVP.
headings: How to read this section Topics Source boundary
title: Future and advanced topics

body: Status: Historical and removed from the accepted product. Marketplaces are not a live feature or an active roadmap lane. The broad OpenAgents thesis imagined a market where agents and people could publish capabilities, contribute data or compute, complete bounded work, and receive verifiable outcomes. Different eras described agent stores, WASM plugin registries, data vending machines, compute providers, developer bounties, and markets for accepted work. What carried across the experiments Capabilities should be inspectable before an agent receives authority to use them. Work should have a bounded request, durable outcome, and evidence trail. Identity, reputation, admission, verification, and settlement are separate authorities. A listing or demo does not prove supply, demand, delivery, or payment. The most durable concept was the accepted outcome : scoped work that is executed, evaluated, receipted, and only then eligible for any later economic action. That idea still informs evidence design, but there is no current market or settlement surface behind it. What was removed The current Desktop MVP does not include agent, plugin, data, compute, labor, or bounty markets. The Node/pnpm/Vite Plus cutover explicitly decommissioned market, payment, wallet, payout, and settlement paths rather than porting them. Historical code and receipts remain evidence only. They do not authorize a listing, purchase, payout, or product claim. Historical source trail The public transcript index groups the arc around the agent store , One Market , data marketplace planning , agent markets , and later compute and data market experiments.
description: The historical OpenAgents thesis for agent, plugin, data, compute, and outcome markets.
headings: What carried across the experiments What was removed Historical source trail
title: Marketplaces

body: Status: Historical protocol work. Nostr is not a live feature of the current Desktop MVP or an active roadmap commitment. Nostr appeared repeatedly as a neutral protocol layer rather than a single product feature. OpenAgents explored it for identity, plugin discovery, key-value storage, data vending, client communication, job requests, and market coordination. Ideas worth retaining User-controlled identity and signing should remain distinct from application accounts. Relay discovery is not the same as service availability or trust. Events need typed schemas, bounded permissions, provenance, and replay rules before they can carry product authority. Open protocols can reduce platform capture, but they do not remove custody, moderation, spam, privacy, or verification problems. The workspace retains shared Nostr engineering in nostr-effect for protocol-level reuse. That repository presence is substrate, not evidence that OpenAgents Desktop exposes Nostr identity, relays, messaging, jobs, or markets. Historical source trail The build-in-public series explored a Nostr plugin registry , Nostr login , Nostr key-value storage , data vending machines , and a Nostr client . Those episodes are historical orientation, not current product documentation.
description: Historical Nostr identity, registry, storage, and open-protocol experiments.
headings: Ideas worth retaining Historical source trail
title: Nostr

body: Status: Historical and removed from the accepted product. Bitcoin and Lightning payments are not a live feature of the current MVP. OpenAgents explored Bitcoin as an open settlement layer for agent work. The work included balance displays, Lightning and L402 payments, paid chat, revenue share, wallet ownership, tipping, provider earnings, and payouts for verified contributions. What the experiments taught A payment request, payment proof, accepted work receipt, and product entitlement are different records. Wallet custody and signing authority must be explicit and recoverable. Idempotency, replay protection, reconciliation, and failure handling matter before any money path can be called live. A demo, invoice, balance display, or historical receipt does not prove a currently supported rail. What was removed Payments, billing credits, wallets, tips, payouts, markets, and settlement sit outside the accepted Desktop MVP. Their executable paths were decommissioned during the toolchain conversion after outstanding value and historical receipts were reconciled. Former records remain read-only evidence; they are not an invitation to send funds. Any revival would need fresh owner approval plus custody, ledger, threat-model, invariant, recovery, and live-proof work. This page provides no wallet address, payment endpoint, or operational instructions. Historical source trail The public series traces the idea through L402 , Lightning withdrawals , agent payments , wallet ownership , and later agent market and tipping demonstrations. They describe past work, not current availability.
description: Historical Bitcoin, Lightning, L402, wallet, tipping, payout, and settlement experiments.
headings: What the experiments taught What was removed Historical source trail
title: Bitcoin and Lightning

body: Status: Dormant design horizon and not part of the current MVP. Remote workrooms are not a live feature or an active follow-on. Earlier OpenAgents work explored controlling coding sessions across Desktop, mobile, owner-managed machines, and managed Agent Computers. Some bounded identity, routing, lifecycle, and evidence contracts landed, but the broader expansion is closed as not planned. Four different promises Remote work often gets compressed into one word, but the roadmap separates four claims: Synchronized conversation — more than one client can view consistent durable state. Remote placement — work can start on a named, authorized target. Restart survival — accepted work survives a process or host restart without silent duplication. Cross-host movement — a session can checkpoint, detach, move, and resume with fresh credentials and no split authority. Proof of one does not prove the others. A mobile UI, remote API, or routed command is not evidence of portable execution. Retained safety boundary If this area is ever reauthorized, targets must remain explicit, owner-controlled and managed targets must follow one lifecycle contract, credentials must use bounded grants, and moves must revoke source authority before minting destination authority. Clients must not silently change provider, custody, account, data posture, or isolation. Fleet, managed targets, portable sessions, mobile expansion, and voice require a new bounded owner decision and issue before work resumes. Historical source trail The public series includes mobile sync , coding agents on a phone , deterministic Fleet delegation , and the later Desktop-focused episodes on predictable software and sub-agent UI . Only the local-first Desktop workroom belongs to the current MVP.
description: The dormant design horizon for Fleet, mobile supervision, remote targets, and portable sessions.
headings: Four different promises Retained safety boundary Historical source trail
title: Remote workrooms
